did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN), specifically a modified Google Inception v4 architecture, to perform diagnostic classification (dichotomous diagnosis of melanoma vs. benign nevi) on dermoscopic images. The CNN's performance was evaluated and compared against 58 dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental data from a comparative cross-sectional reader study involving a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) and 58 dermatologists, including specific Methods, Results (sensitivity, specificity, AUC values), and Conclusions based on new data collection and analysis.

title: Man against machine: diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network for dermoscopic melanoma recognition in comparison to 58 dermatologists
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) for dermoscopic melanoma recognition compared to 58 dermatologists. The researchers trained a modified Google Inception v4 CNN on dermoscopic images and tested it against a 100-image test set in a comparative cross-sectional reader study. The CNN's performance was assessed using sensitivity, specificity, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for dichotomous classification of melanoma versus benign nevi. The CNN achieved a higher specificity (82.5%) and a greater ROC AUC (0.86) than the mean performance of the dermatologists (specificity 71.3% to 75.7% and AUC 0.79). The results indicate that the CNN outperformed most dermatologists, including experts, in classifying melanocytic lesions from dermoscopic images. The study concludes that CNNs can serve as effective aids for physicians in melanoma detection regardless of their experience level.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dichotomous classification task (melanoma vs. benign nevus). The test sets included 20% melanomas (in situ and invasive) of all body sites and frequent histotypes. The CNN's malignancy score was used to classify these.

Included in the dichotomous classification task (melanoma vs. benign nevus). The test sets included 80% benign melanocytic nevi of different subtypes and body sites, including 'melanoma simulators'. Most were non-excised lesions validated by follow-up.

all_task_types.target: Dichotomous diagnosis of melanoma versus benign nevi
all_task_types.details: The CNN performed a dichotomous classification of dermoscopic images into malignant (melanoma) or benign (nevi) categories. The model output a 'malignancy score' ranging from 0 to 1, with a cutoff of > 0.5 used for the binary classification. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and ROC AUC for this diagnostic classification task.

datasets_used.name: Test-set-300
datasets_used.description: A 300-image dataset created by the authors, retrieved from the high-quality validated image library of the Department of Dermatology, University of Heidelberg, Germany. It included 20% melanomas (in situ and invasive) of all body sites and frequent histotypes, and 80% benign melanocytic nevi of different subtypes and body sites, including 'melanoma simulators'. Most benign nevi were non-excised lesions validated by follow-up examinations.

use.training: Used to create the subset for testing (Test-set-100). No overlap between datasets for training/validation and testing was allowed.
use.testing: Used to derive the 100-image test-set (Test-set-100) for the comparative reader study. Also used for internal performance evaluation of the CNN (sensitivity 95%, specificity 80%, AUC 0.95).

datasets_used.name: Test-set-100
datasets_used.description: A 100-image subset selected prospectively by two experienced dermatologists from Test-set-300 for an increased diagnostic difficulty. It was used for the comparative cross-sectional reader study involving 58 dermatologists.

use.testing: Used as the primary test set for the comparative reader study (Level-I and Level-II) against 58 dermatologists and for CNN performance evaluation (sensitivity 95%, specificity 63.8%, AUC 0.86).

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A 100-image dataset created by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the 2016 International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) challenge. Used for external comparison of the CNN's performance against the top-five algorithms of the challenge.

use.testing: Used for external comparison of the CNN's ROC AUC against the top-five ranked algorithms of the 2016 ISBI challenge.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC)
Management decision metrics (excision rate, short-term follow-up rate, number needed to excise (NNE))

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the CNN's specificity at the level of the average dermatologist sensitivity and the CNN's ROC AUC versus the mean ROC area of dermatologists.
To assess differences in the dermatologists’ diagnostic performance between level-I and level-II of the reader study.
To summarize data using frequency, mean, range, and standard deviation.
To evaluate diagnostic classification performance using Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the CNN and dermatologists.
To determine statistical significance for hypothesis tests.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the diagnostic performance (specificity or ROC AUC) between the CNN and the dermatologists, or between dermatologists' performance at level-I and level-II.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the diagnostic performance (specificity or ROC AUC) between the CNN and the dermatologists, or between dermatologists' performance at level-I and level-II.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined at the P < 0.05 level. The CNN's specificity was significantly higher than dermatologists' at both level-I (P < 0.01) and level-II (P < 0.01). The CNN's ROC AUC was significantly greater than the mean ROC area of dermatologists (P < 0.01). Additionally, dermatologists' specificity significantly improved from level-I to level-II (P < 0.05), and experts' ROC area was significantly better than beginners' in level-II (P = 0.03).

Test-set-100.description: 100-image subset selected for increased diagnostic difficulty, used for comparative reader study (Level-I and Level-II) and CNN evaluation.
Test-set-100.notes: The CNN's specificity (82.5%) was significantly higher than dermatologists' at their mean sensitivities in both Level-I (71.3%, P < 0.01) and Level-II (75.7%, P < 0.01). The CNN's ROC AUC (0.86) was greater than the mean ROC area of dermatologists (0.79 in Level-I, 0.82 in Level-II, P < 0.01). Thirteen (22.4%) of 58 dermatologists showed slightly higher diagnostic performance than the CNN in Level-I.

Test-set-300.description: 300-image dataset including 20% melanomas and 80% benign nevi, used for internal CNN performance evaluation.
Test-set-300.notes: This set included less difficult-to-diagnose lesions compared to Test-set-100. The CNN's melanoma probability scores were significantly different between benign lesions and in situ/invasive melanomas (P < 0.001).

ISBI 2016 Challenge Dataset.description: 100-image dataset from the 2016 International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging challenge, used for external comparison.
ISBI 2016 Challenge Dataset.notes: The CNN scored results close to the top three algorithms of the ISBI 2016 challenge, with an ROC AUC of 0.7868, placing it among the top three ranked algorithms.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained GoogleNet Inception CNN architecture trained with more than 100,000 digital images and corresponding disease labels

architecture_details.modification: Modified version of Google's Inception v4 CNN architecture

training_details.task: Diagnostic classification of dermoscopic images of lesions of melanocytic origin (melanoma, benign nevi)

classification_or_output_head.threshold: > 0.5 for dichotomous classification of malignant versus benign lesions

preprocessing_techniques: The CNN was not restricted by man-made segmentation criteria, deconstructing digital images down to the pixel level.
The CNN was trained on dermoscopic images and corresponding diagnoses from Test-set-300.
The CNN was tested on Test-set-100, a subset of Test-set-300 selected by two experienced dermatologists for increased diagnostic difficulty.
The CNN was evaluated on the ISBI 2016 Challenge Dataset (100 images) for external comparison.
conclusions: The deep learning convolutional neural network (CNN) outperformed the mean diagnostic performance of 58 dermatologists, including 30 experts, in the dichotomous classification of dermoscopic images of melanoma versus benign nevi.

The CNN achieved a significantly higher specificity (82.5%) compared to dermatologists at their respective mean sensitivities in both level-I (dermoscopy only, 71.3%, P < 0.01) and level-II (dermoscopy plus clinical information, 75.7%, P < 0.01).
The CNN's area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) was significantly greater than the mean AUC of dermatologists (0.86 versus 0.79 in level-I and 0.82 in level-II, P < 0.01).

While additional clinical information improved dermatologists' diagnostic performance (sensitivity increased from 86.6% to 88.9% and specificity from 71.3% to 75.7%), their specificity remained inferior to that of the CNN.
The CNN's performance was comparable to the top three algorithms of the 2016 International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) challenge, with an ROC AUC of 0.7868.

Thirteen (22.4%) of the 58 dermatologists showed slightly higher diagnostic performance than the CNN in level-I, indicating that while the CNN outperformed most, it did not outperform all dermatologists.
The study concludes that CNNs can serve as effective aids for physicians in melanoma detection regardless of their individual level of experience and training.

The authors acknowledge that differences in the level of difficulty inherent to any image test-set directly impact diagnostic performance, highlighting the importance of using large, diverse groups of dermatologists and open source datasets for future comparisons.
important_remarks: The study setting was artificial as dermatologists did not face the real-world consequences of missing a melanoma, potentially inflating their performance compared to clinical practice.

The test sets did not include the full range of differential diagnoses, specifically excluding pigmented basal cell carcinoma and seborrheic keratosis, limiting generalizability to broader dermatological practice.
There was a shortage of validated images from other skin types and genetic backgrounds, introducing potential population bias.
Thirteen (22.4%) of the 58 dermatologists outperformed the CNN in Level-I, indicating that the CNN did not outperform all experts.

The CNN was trained and validated on data from the University of Heidelberg, Germany, and tested on a subset of the same institution's library, raising concerns about data leakage or lack of external validation for the training process.
The study acknowledges that poor availability of validated images from diverse populations may limit the broader generalization of the CNN's performance.
Clinical implementation may be hindered by the fact that operating physicians may not follow CNN recommendations if they do not fully trust the algorithm.
